seo.title: Si Sa Ket Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Explore Si Sa Ket Province: Khao Phra Wihan (Preah Vihear) UNESCO temple, Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai Khmer ruins, Pha Mo I Daeng cliff views over Cambodia. Area: 8,840 km², 22 districts. Plan your Isaan visit.

overview: Si Sa Ket Province occupies the southeastern corner of the Khorat Plateau in Isaan, sharing a long border with Cambodia to the south. Though one of Thailand's lesser-visited northeastern provinces, it punches well above its weight for heritage tourism: Khao Phra Wihan (the Thai name for the UNESCO-listed Preah Vihear temple) crowns a dramatic cliff on the Cambodian border and can be accessed from the Thai side via a national park entrance point and a stunning cliff-edge viewpoint at Pha Mo I Daeng. Closer to the provincial capital, the 11th-century Khmer sanctuary of Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai is one of the finest and most easily accessible Khmer monuments in Isaan, its pink-sandstone towers rising from a tree-shaded courtyard that few foreign tourists ever discover. The province is also Thailand's largest producer of garlic and shallots — fields of both cover the flat countryside around Kantharalak and the Mun River valley, and the annual harvest season (December to February) fills roadside markets with fragrant fresh produce. Si Sa Ket's 22 districts support a predominantly agricultural economy anchored by jasmine rice, silk weaving, and the indigenous Suay (Kuay) community's unique cultural traditions, most visibly celebrated at the annual elephant festival in Surin next door.
geography: Si Sa Ket covers 8,840 km² on the southeastern Khorat Plateau, bordered by Cambodia along its southern edge where the Dongrak Mountain Range forms a natural and political boundary. The Mun River — one of the Mekong's major tributaries — flows westward through the heart of the province, draining the broad alluvial plain that supports the region's intensive rice and garlic cultivation. The terrain is predominantly flat to gently rolling agricultural land at 120–200 metres elevation, rising abruptly at the Dongrak escarpment in the south. The Dongrak Range's vertical sandstone cliffs drop hundreds of metres into Cambodian territory — this dramatic edge is the setting for both the Khao Phra Wihan temple complex and the Pha Mo I Daeng viewpoint, where the plateau simply ends at a sheer cliff with sweeping views across the Cambodian lowlands. The provincial climate follows the classic Isaan savanna pattern: a cool dry season (November–February), a brutal hot season (March–May regularly exceeding 38°C), and a monsoon season (June–October) that turns the paddy fields brilliant green.
highlights: Khao Phra Wihan (Preah Vihear) National Park — access the Thai-side viewpoints of the disputed UNESCO World Heritage temple perched on the Dongrak escarpment above Cambodia
Pha Mo I Daeng — a dramatic cliff-edge viewpoint on the Cambodian border with sheer drops and panoramic views over the Cambodian plains hundreds of metres below
Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai — one of Isaan's finest Khmer temple complexes, with elegant pink-sandstone prangs and intricate lintels in a peaceful forest setting near the capital
Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Noi — a smaller but well-preserved Khmer sanctuary companion site a few kilometres from Sa Kamphaeng Yai
Garlic and shallot harvest markets — from December to February, roadside markets around Kantharalak overflow with fresh garlic and shallots from Thailand's largest production zone
Kaeng Tana National Park on the Mun River — rocky rapids, islets, and forest trails on the border with Ubon Ratchathani
Sam Phan Bok — seasonal rocky riverbed formations on the Mekong in neighbouring Ubon, often visited as a day trip from Si Sa Ket in October–November
Silk weaving villages — hand-woven mudmee ikat silk produced in village cooperatives throughout the province, sold at local markets and the OTOP provincial centre

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool dry season with temperatures of 18–30°C. Ideal for exploring Khmer ruins and the cliff viewpoints. The garlic harvest fills markets from December onward. Visibility from Pha Mo I Daeng is at its clearest.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures regularly reaching 38–40°C. The Songkran festival in April enlivens the capital. Temple visits are best done early morning. The border viewpoints remain accessible but heat can be intense.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon rains turn the province lush and green, with rice fields at their most photogenic. Pha Mo I Daeng and the Dongrak escarpment can be misty and dramatic in the rains. Kaeng Tana rapids are at full flow. Expect muddy rural roads.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the most comfortable conditions: cool weather, clear views from the cliff viewpoints, and the added spectacle of the garlic and shallot harvest markets. Avoid the hot season if hiking is on your itinerary.

topAttractions.name: Khao Phra Wihan National Park (Preah Vihear Viewpoints)
topAttractions.description: The Thai entry point to the Dongrak escarpment that overlooks the UNESCO World Heritage Preah Vihear temple complex. The temple itself sits on the Cambodian side of the border, but a national park area on the Thai side provides cliff-edge viewpoints and the dramatic Pha Mo I Daeng lookout. The temple was the subject of a prolonged Thailand–Cambodia territorial dispute and armed standoffs in 2008–2011; the Thai-side access and Cambodian visitor arrangements have varied over the years — check current border status before visiting. The viewpoints alone are among the most spectacular natural scenery in Isaan.
topAttractions.entranceFee: National park entrance: 200 THB foreigners, 40 THB Thais. Open daily (subject to border status)

sources.sourceName: Thailand National Parks — Khao Phra Wihan

topAttractions.name: Pha Mo I Daeng
topAttractions.description: A breathtaking cliff-edge viewpoint within Khao Phra Wihan National Park, where the Dongrak escarpment drops vertically into the Cambodian lowlands hundreds of metres below. On clear days the view extends across an apparently infinite flat plain stretching deep into Cambodia. The cliff itself — a sheer sandstone wall — is geologically dramatic and photographers gather here at sunrise and sunset. The name translates roughly as 'painted rock face,' and the area also has ancient rock paintings nearby. The combination of geological spectacle and cross-border panorama makes this one of the most memorable viewpoints in all of Isaan.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Included in Khao Phra Wihan National Park entrance (200 THB foreigners)

topAttractions.name: Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai
topAttractions.description: The most important and best-preserved Khmer monument in Si Sa Ket Province, located in Uthumphon Phisai District about 50 km north of the capital. Built in the early Angkor period (11th–12th century), the complex consists of three pink-sandstone prangs on a laterite base, surrounded by a moat and enclosure walls. The central prang — dedicated to Shiva — features beautifully carved lintels and pediments showing scenes from Hindu mythology. The site sits within a quiet temple precinct that functions as an active Buddhist monastery, giving it a living, layered atmosphere very different from the more polished tourist sites. One of Isaan's underrated architectural gems.

sources.sourceName: Wikipedia — Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai

topAttractions.name: Prasat Hin Wat Sa Kamphaeng Noi
topAttractions.description: A smaller companion Khmer sanctuary a few kilometres from its famous neighbour Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai, built in a similar early Angkor style with a single laterite and sandstone prang. Less visited and entirely uncrowded, it offers a contemplative experience of Khmer heritage in a rural Isaan setting. The site is freely accessible and sits within the grounds of a functioning village temple — local monks and devotees share the space with the ancient stones, creating an authentic atmosphere that polished archaeological parks rarely achieve.

topAttractions.name: Kaeng Tana National Park
topAttractions.description: A scenic national park straddling the Mun River on Si Sa Ket's border with Ubon Ratchathani Province, featuring rocky rapids, small river islets (don), forested trails, and riverside camping. The park is particularly beautiful during and just after the monsoon season (September–November) when the Mun River runs high and the vegetation is lush. In the dry season (January–April), the receding river exposes rock formations and sandbars popular with picnicking Thai families. Wildlife includes river otters, monitor lizards, and numerous bird species along the riparian corridor.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB foreigners, 100 THB Thais. Camping available from 30 THB/person

localFood: Khanom Jeen Nam Ya Si Sa Ket — fresh rice noodles with a local version of the southern-style curry fish sauce, served with a wide array of vegetable sides; a staple breakfast across the province
Larb Moo Isaan — minced pork larb with toasted rice powder, dried chillies, shallots, and fresh mint; the fiery Isaan salad eaten with sticky rice and raw vegetables
Kai Yang Kantharalak — charcoal-grilled garlic-marinated chicken from the garlic-growing heartland of Kantharalak District, with a distinctively pungent, caramelised crust
Som Tam Poo Pla Ra — green papaya salad with fermented freshwater fish (pla ra) and preserved crab; the authentic Isaan version that defines the region's cuisine
Khao Niao — steamed glutinous sticky rice, the daily staple of the province, hand-rolled into balls and eaten with every meal
Krathiam Dow Si Sa Ket — deep-fried whole garlic cloves from local farms, eaten as a snack or condiment; a uniquely regional treat found at harvest-season markets
Pla Nin Pao — whole salt-crusted grilled tilapia from Mun River fish farms, served with a spicy tamarind dipping sauce and fresh herbs
Tom Saep — the Isaan-style hot and sour pork rib soup with galangal, lemongrass, and a sharp tamarind base — more intensely flavoured and spicier than the central Thai version
culture: Si Sa Ket's cultural fabric is woven from three threads: its deep Khmer archaeological heritage, the indigenous Suay (Kuay) people's living traditions, and the agricultural rhythms of Thailand's garlic and shallot heartland. The province contains dozens of Khmer-era prasat ruins that served as way-stations along the ancient Angkor road network — most famously the sandstone towers of Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai and the cliff-top UNESCO temple of Preah Vihear visible from the escarpment. The Suay community, scattered across several districts, maintains traditions of elephant mahoutry (historically supplying the royal elephant corps) and distinctive animist-Buddhist ceremonies rarely seen elsewhere. The annual garlic and shallot harvest from December to February transforms the roadside economy: trucks laden with fresh-pulled garlic and purple shallots queue at sorting sheds, and market stalls piled high with braided garlic strings appear at every junction. Mor lam music, hand-woven silk, and Bun Bung Fai (rocket festival) celebrations connect Si Sa Ket firmly to the wider Isaan cultural sphere.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, Si Sa Ket is served by daily trains on the northeastern rail line from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong or Bang Sue Grand Station — the journey takes approximately 8–9 hours on rapid or express services, with fares from 200–450 THB; Si Sa Ket has its own train station in the city centre. Buses depart Bangkok's Mo Chit (Northern) Bus Terminal with services operated by Nakhon Chai Air and other companies; the journey takes 7–9 hours with fares of 300–600 THB. There is no commercial airport in Si Sa Ket — the nearest airports are at Ubon Ratchathani (90 km east, served by Thai Airways, Bangkok Airways, and Nok Air with daily flights from Bangkok) and Surin (90 km west, with limited services). From Ubon Ratchathani airport, buses and minivans connect to Si Sa Ket in approximately 90 minutes. By car from Bangkok on Highway 24 via Nakhon Ratchasima, the drive takes approximately 7–8 hours.
gettingAround: Si Sa Ket city is compact and navigable by foot or tuk-tuk, but reaching the province's major attractions requires private or hired transport. Renting a motorbike (200–300 THB/day) in the city is the best option for exploring Khmer ruins and rural garlic farms. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) connect the capital to district towns including Kantharalak (the gateway for Khao Phra Wihan and Pha Mo I Daeng). For the cliff viewpoints and national park, shared minivans or hired songthaews depart from Kantharalak bus station in the morning — confirm return times carefully as services are infrequent. Tuk-tuks and motorbike taxis cover the city centre. Grab ride-hailing has limited availability in Si Sa Ket city. Organised day tours from Si Sa Ket or Kantharalak to the national park are the most reliable option for visitors without their own transport.
travelTips: Check the current border access status at Khao Phra Wihan before travelling — the Thai-Cambodian border situation has periodically affected access; the Tourism Authority of Thailand and park websites carry up-to-date information
Arrive at Pha Mo I Daeng at sunrise for the most dramatic lighting and clearest views across the Cambodian plains — afternoon haze can reduce visibility significantly
The garlic and shallot harvest season (December–February) is a uniquely Si Sa Ket experience — visit the sorting markets around Kantharalak early morning when the day's harvest arrives
Wat Sa Kamphaeng Yai is free to enter and is located within an active Buddhist monastery — dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered) and maintain a quiet, respectful demeanour
Kantharalak town (85 km south of Si Sa Ket city) makes a convenient overnight base for the Khao Phra Wihan national park and Pha Mo I Daeng viewpoint
Carry cash outside the provincial capital — ATMs are rare near the national park and border area; stock up before leaving Kantharalak or Si Sa Ket city
The Kaeng Tana national park rapids are best visited in October–November when the Mun River is still running high after the monsoon but the rains have stopped

Si Sa Ket's silk is woven in village cooperatives near the capital — the provincial OTOP centre on the main road is the best single location to compare styles and buy directly from weavers at fair prices

sources.sourceName: Thailand National Parks — Khao Phra Wihan
